tipRules.tip: Johnston: When you get bit on a moving bait like a swimbait, work around the area with a tube or drop-shot to pick off additional fish from the same spot.

Johnston: There probably isn't a better bait for smallmouths than the tube since it imitates everything a smallmouth loves to eat, from perch to gobies to crawfish. If the bottom is clean, you can't beat a tube.

knowledge.insight: Johnston: Cover water quickly with a moving bait like a crankbait or swimbait. If you drifted across the whole lake you could catch bass everywhere - that's why you need to cover water and find the sweet spots where they are grouped up.
Johnston: Keep rod choices simple on Lake St. Clair - two swimbaits, a tube, and a drop-shot are all you need on the deck.
Johnston: If shallow water has a chalky tint to it, head back out to the lake. Water color changes indicate you need to move to cleaner water.
Johnston: There are so many smallmouths in St. Clair that you can catch them anywhere, even in shallow pencil reeds where you'd expect largemouth. A 4-pound smallmouth in reeds is not unusual.
